network.overall_strategy: The network centers valine synthesis on the best-supported prebiotic route: Strecker chemistry from isobutyraldehyde, ammonia, and hydrogen cyanide to the valine aminonitrile, followed by hydrolysis to racemic valine. To satisfy the literature, the network explicitly treats branched C4 precursor supply as the bottleneck rather than overstating its accessibility. Multiple upstream routes converge on isobutyraldehyde or on the alternative hub alpha-ketoisovalerate: (i) hydrothermal/surface low-selectivity aldol diversification from acetaldehyde under NiS-related vent chemistry, (ii) spark/discharge-like surface inventory transfer represented through HCN-rich pools with aldehyde mixtures, (iii) Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin branching under CO2-rich cyanide chemistry, and (iv) metabolically congruent reductive amination of alpha-ketoisovalerate under FeS/pyrite/Ni conditions. Hydrothermal reactions are constrained to moderate alkaline-vent regimes rather than destructive black-smoker conditions, while surface pools provide HCN concentration, wet-dry cycling, borate/clay concentration, and photochemical aminonitrile protection. Biochemical/prebiotic steps complete hydrolysis, formyl deprotection, and peptide incorporation. Several reactions are marked speculative where the literature explicitly identifies unresolved gaps, especially selective abiotic formation of isobutyraldehyde and alpha-ketoisovalerate.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction to formaldehyde/acetaldehyde inventory

conditions.catalyst: magnetite and Fe0 under Fischer-Tropsch-type vent conditions
conditions.other: anoxic, reducing hydrothermal flow; moderate vent chemistry favored over prolonged >573K exposure

reactions.mechanism: CO2/CO hydrogenation on Fe-bearing mineral surfaces generates reduced C1-C2 carbonyl compounds including formaldehyde and acetaldehyde as part of a mixed product inventory.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis on Fe/Ni minerals generates hydrocarbons, alcohols, and aldehydes rather than amino acids directly, providing plausible upstream carbonyl feedstocks for later Strecker chemistry (McCollom, Ritter & Simoneit, 1999). The network keeps this as an inventory-generating step rather than a selective aldehyde synthesis, consistent with the literature bottleneck discussion.

reactions.name: Surface cyanide inventory generation in UV/discharge-accessible pools

conditions.catalyst: TiO2-assisted photochemistry with sulfur-bearing mineral context
conditions.other: UV-irradiated, anoxic surface pools; represents atmospheric/discharge-derived cyanide captured in water rather than a clean single-step stoichiometric synthesis

reactions.mechanism: Photochemical and discharge-accessible carbon-nitrogen chemistry yields HCN-rich and formamide-bearing surface inventories from reduced atmospheric organics and NH3, with TiO2 aiding photoredox processing and formamide accumulation.
reactions.reasoning: The literature requires avoiding an unrealistic direct CO2 + NH3 -> HCN step. HCN is well established in spark/discharge systems and formamide in UV/cyanide chemistry; TiO2 is a plausible surface photocatalyst for prebiotic photochemistry and formamide transformations (Miller, 1953; Johnson et al., 2008; Parker et al., 2011; Saladino et al., 2006). This node intentionally represents capture/concentration of a known atmospheric photochemical inventory rather than an overclaimed selective synthesis.

reactions.name: Low-selectivity aldol diversification to branched aldehydes including isobutyraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: transition-metal sulfide-rich mineral surfaces with concentration on clay
conditions.other: wet-dry cycling; low selectivity; product represented as the branched aldehyde fraction of a broader aldol mixture

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde undergoes aldol diversification and rearrangement within complex aldehyde mixtures, producing trace branched C4 aldehydes including isobutyraldehyde among many linear and over-condensed products.
reactions.reasoning: This step directly addresses prior failures by avoiding an unrealistic selective acetaldehyde-to-isobutyraldehyde conversion. The literature identifies branched aldehyde formation as low-selectivity and unresolved, with NiS-catalyzed acetylene/aldehyde chemistry and spark-generated radical mixtures producing higher aldehydes including branched species only as minor components (Preiner et al., 2023; Parker et al., 2011). This reaction is therefore explicitly speculative-but-necessary and low-flux.

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate formation from reduced carbon

conditions.other: pH 9-11, H2-rich, serpentinization-fed, moderate alkaline vent window

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-assisted CO2 reduction and carbon-carbon bond formation generate alpha-keto acid inventory including pyruvate under reducing alkaline vent conditions.
reactions.reasoning: The instructions require inclusion of hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis, but the literature for valine emphasizes moderate alkaline vents rather than extreme temperatures. This step is used as a plausible hydrothermal alpha-keto acid hub consistent with mineral-mediated protometabolic carbon fixation scenarios and hydrothermal thermodynamic favorability of amino acid synthesis (Amend & Shock, 1998; Martin & Russell, 2007; Muchowska, Varma & Moran, 2019). It remains less directly validated than the Strecker core.

reactions.name: Speculative protometabolic formation of alpha-ketoisovalerate from pyruvate and acetaldehyde equivalents

conditions.other: alkaline to near-neutral vent micropores; intended as a low-confidence condensation analogue to acetolactate/keto-acid chemistry

reactions.mechanism: Nonenzymatic carbon-carbon coupling in a pyruvate-rich hydrothermal inventory yields a branched alpha-keto acid pool represented here by alpha-ketoisovalerate.
reactions.reasoning: The literature identifies alpha-ketoisovalerate as a metabolically congruent alternative precursor to valine but explicitly notes that its prebiotic formation remains experimentally undemonstrated. This reaction is therefore included as a clearly speculative connector grounded in protometabolic alpha-keto acid logic rather than direct validation (Copley, Smith & Morowitz, 2005; Muchowska, Varma & Moran, 2019; Krishnamurthy group, 2025 PMC). It should be interpreted as a weak branch, not a high-confidence route.

reactions.name: Mineral-mediated reductive amination of alpha-ketoisovalerate to valine

conditions.catalyst: FeS/H2S-pyrite-forming system or metallic Ni analog
conditions.other: alkaline pH ~8-10; moderate hydrothermal conditions favored; analogous Ni-catalyzed chemistry possible at 295K

reactions.mechanism: Alpha-ketoisovalerate condenses with ammonia to an imine/iminium intermediate followed by mineral-mediated reduction to racemic valine.
reactions.reasoning: Reductive amination of alpha-keto acids is experimentally established on FeS/H2S systems and revisited with freshly precipitated FeS/Fe(OH)2; modern work extends the chemistry to biological keto acids with H2 on metallic Ni, while mixed-valence iron minerals also promote alpha-keto acid amination. Specific alpha-ketoisovalerate data are limited, so extension to valine is plausible but still partly inferential (Hafenbradl et al., 1995; Huber & Wächtershäuser, 2003; Barge et al., 2019; Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Canonical Strecker synthesis of valine aminonitrile from isobutyraldehyde

conditions.catalyst: mineral concentration rather than strict catalysis; borate/clay aid concentration and buffering
conditions.other: pH ~9 for free NH3 availability; cyanide-rich evaporitic or pond setting

reactions.mechanism: Isobutyraldehyde forms an imine with NH3; cyanide adds to the imine to yield the alpha-aminonitrile 2-amino-3-methylbutanenitrile.
reactions.reasoning: This is the best-supported prebiotic route to valine. The mechanism and aldehyde identity are directly supported by Miller and Van Trump's mechanistic work, Keefe et al.'s identification of isobutyraldehyde as the valine Strecker precursor, and first-principles thermodynamic analysis of the Strecker free-energy landscape (Miller & Van Trump, 1981; Keefe, Newton & Miller, 1995; Devergne, Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta, 2021). The pH requirement near 9 follows the literature report that free NH3 availability is critical.

reactions.name: Formamide-mediated N-formyl protection of valine aminonitrile

conditions.catalyst: formamide-rich drying films on mineral surfaces

reactions.mechanism: Aminonitrile N-formylation in formamide-rich media suppresses reversal of Strecker equilibria and stabilizes the aminonitrile pool.
reactions.reasoning: N-formylation of aminonitriles in formamide was shown to protect them from retro-Strecker reversion and can improve amino acid yields after later hydrolysis. TiO2 and phosphate minerals are included as plausible surface agents for formamide-rich prebiotic films without overstating catalytic specificity (Green, Xu & Sutherland, 2021; Green, Xu & Sutherland, 2023; Saladino et al., 2006).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of valine aminonitrile to valine

conditions.other: long-lived aqueous pools; hydrolysis irreversible relative to upstream Strecker equilibria

reactions.mechanism: Sequential nitrile hydration and hydrolysis via amide-like intermediates yields racemic valine, driving the Strecker sequence forward.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrolysis of aminonitriles to amino acids is the terminal irreversible step of the Strecker pathway and is central to valine formation in Miller chemistry. Aminonitrile hydrolysis timescales are compatible with long prebiotic residence times, especially at modest temperatures (Miller & Van Trump, 1981; Miller, 1998; Devergne, Magrino, Pietrucci & Saitta, 2021).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis/deprotection of N-formyl valine aminonitrile to valine

conditions.other: rehydration after dry phases; gradual deprotection and nitrile hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Rehydration removes the formyl protecting group and hydrolyzes the nitrile to the amino acid, preserving flux from stabilized aminonitrile reservoirs into valine.
reactions.reasoning: This reaction follows from the demonstrated protective role of aminonitrile formylation in formamide systems; deprotection/hydrolysis is a reasonable downstream fate in aqueous prebiotic pools, though less directly quantified for valine than the protection step itself (Green, Xu & Sutherland, 2021; Green, Xu & Sutherland, 2023).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs-type conversion of isobutyraldehyde to valine hydantoin

conditions.catalyst: carbonate/CO2-rich cyanide chemistry on concentrating mineral surfaces
conditions.other: CO2-bearing evaporitic pool; cyanide and ammonia present; alternative to direct Strecker hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Carbonyl compound, cyanide, ammonia, and CO2/carbonate chemistry funnels through a hydantoin intermediate, bridging aldehyde-based Strecker chemistry and alpha-keto acid-like amino acid access.
reactions.reasoning: The literature specifically identifies Bucherer-Bergs chemistry as a relevant variant when CO2 is present alongside HCN and NH3, linking aldehyde chemistry to hydantoin intermediates. It is included here as a supported alternative branch rather than the dominant route (Pulletikurti, Yadav, Springsteen & Krishnamurthy, 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of valine hydantoin to valine

conditions.other: repeated heating-cooling or long aqueous residence; slower and more condition-sensitive than direct aminonitrile hydrolysis

reactions.mechanism: Hydantoin ring opening and hydrolysis yields valine under prolonged aqueous processing.
reactions.reasoning: Hydantoin intermediates are established within Bucherer-Bergs amino acid synthesis, though hydrolysis is more demanding than direct aminonitrile hydrolysis. This is therefore an auxiliary but literature-grounded route to valine (Pulletikurti, Yadav, Springsteen & Krishnamurthy, 2022).

reactions.name: Wet-dry clay-mediated oligomerization of valine to valylvaline

conditions.other: repeated wet-dry cycles in mineral-rich pools; stoichiometrically represents self-condensation of two valine molecules

reactions.mechanism: Concentration during dehydration enables ester-mediated or surface-assisted amide bond formation between valine molecules, yielding a dipeptide pool represented by valylvaline.
reactions.reasoning: This downstream step shows functional incorporation of valine into prebiotic peptides in evaporative settings. Wet-dry cycling is experimentally established for peptide formation, though specific valylvaline yield is not directly reported here; thus this is a plausible extension beyond the target synthesis itself (Forsythe et al., 2015; Campbell et al., 2019).

pathways.name: Canonical Strecker route from hydrothermal aldehydes
pathways.description: Hydrothermal reduced-carbon chemistry supplies acetaldehyde, low-selectivity surface aldol diversification yields trace isobutyraldehyde, and the validated Strecker sequence forms valine via aminonitrile hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Protected Strecker route via N-formyl aminonitrile
pathways.description: Same core Strecker route, but aminonitrile is stabilized by formamide-mediated N-formylation before delayed hydrolysis to valine.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin route from branched aldehyde
pathways.description: CO2-bearing cyanide chemistry converts isobutyraldehyde into a valine hydantoin intermediate that hydrolyzes to valine.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal keto-acid reductive amination route
pathways.description: A metabolically congruent alternative where hydrothermal pyruvate chemistry feeds speculative alpha-ketoisovalerate formation, followed by mineral-mediated reductive amination to valine.

pathways.name: Mixed hydrothermal-surface Strecker route with pyruvate-supported carbon inventory
pathways.description: Hydrothermal vent chemistry supplies both pyruvate and acetaldehyde inventories; surface cyanide pools then complete the canonical Strecker branch.

pathways.name: Surface-heavy Strecker route under cyanide concentration
pathways.description: Surface pools rich in HCN/formamide intercept branched aldehyde traces from aldol diversification and channel them through the Strecker sequence.

pathways.name: Surface-heavy protected Strecker route
pathways.description: HCN/formamide-rich surface pools not only support Strecker aminonitrile formation but also preserve that intermediate through N-formylation before later hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Alternative hydantoin branch from mixed carbon sources
pathways.description: Hydrothermal acetaldehyde plus surface cyanide/CO2 chemistry yields valine through the hydantoin detour rather than direct aminonitrile hydrolysis.

pathways.name: Keto-acid route followed by peptide incorporation
pathways.description: A weaker but metabolically relevant pathway in which valine formed by reductive amination enters downstream peptide-forming wet-dry chemistry.

pathways.name: Canonical Strecker route followed by peptide incorporation
pathways.description: The highest-confidence valine synthesis route is extended into biochemical assembly by wet-dry peptide formation.
